summary.overall: Chapter 6 content is generally accurate and operationally sound but suffers from pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding across questions and answer-position bias in many multiple-choice items. Source fidelity is strong with few factual errors. Explanation quality is adequate but often restates answers rather than teaching controlling principles.
patterns: Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 83 of 85 question stems reference paragraph numbers (e.g., "Per 6-1-1," "Under 6-2-1c2"), teaching document navigation trivia rather than operational decision-making.
Correct-answer-first bias: 32 of 85 questions place the correct answer in the first position, providing an answer-position cue that undermines assessment validity.
Answer-length cues: 10 questions have correct answers conspicuously longer than distractors, inadvertently signaling the right answer.
Fill-in-the-blank contextual deficits: Single-number fill-in-the-blank questions test rote recall of minima without sufficient operational context to connect the number to its application.
Negative stems: 14 questions flagged for negative stems; most are acceptable as they mirror source-text negation (e.g., "not received"), but a few construct unnecessarily confusing double-negatives.

Flashcard format strength: Most flashcards are well-structured with concept-focused fronts and concise backs, though most include paragraph labels as organizational prefixes rather than retrieval targets.
Activity quality: The 5 activities show good operational scenario design, but explanations contain paragraph-number references and two have answer-position bias.
Cross-format reinforcement: Several source statements (notably in 6-1-4 and 6-1-5) are well-covered by questions, flashcards, and activities, but this is inconsistent across the chapter.
generation_guidance: Eliminate all paragraph-number references from question stems. Teach the concept, not its location in the document.
Randomize choice order to avoid answer-position bias. The correct answer should not be consistently first.
Ensure distractor and correct-answer lengths are comparable. Operational distractors do not need to be wordy to be plausible.
For fill-in-the-blank minima questions, wrap the number recall in operational decision context rather than paragraph-citation context.
Retain exact recall requirements for phraseology (CROSS fix AT OR BEFORE time, MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES, USE DME DISTANCES) as these are operational requirements.
For flashcards, continue using paragraph labels as organizational prefixes on threshold/procedure cards, but ensure concept cards ask about the concept without document navigation.
When generating explanations, teach the controlling principle (e.g., why the minimum exists, what operational condition triggers it) rather than restating which subparagraph contains the answer.

Ensure multi-tier minima (e.g., 6-1-5 arrival minima with different values for same runway vs parallel/crossing) are reinforced across all three formats to build strong operational pattern recognition.
For activities, embed the source paragraph reference only when it helps the learner find the information in the .65 — never as a substitute for operational context.
Review negative stems carefully. "Which is NOT" questions should only be used when exclusion recognition is the actual operational skill being tested.
